title: The Water Cycle
puzzle: Why does Earth almost never run out of the same water?
insight: Water constantly moves through evaporation, condensation, precipitation, and flow, cycling between sky, land, and sea instead of staying fixed in one place.
key_ideas: Rain and runoff return water to land and ocean.
mind_blower: The water you drink may have passed through clouds, rivers, glaciers, dinosaurs, and oceans across immense spans of time.
next_curiosity: If water is constantly recycled, why do some places still face severe water scarcity?
narration_text: Why does Earth almost never run out of the same water? Water constantly moves through evaporation, condensation, precipitation, and flow, cycling between sky, land, and sea instead of staying fixed in one place. Sunlight drives evaporation. Cooling turns vapor into clouds. Rain and runoff return water to land and ocean. The water you drink may have passed through clouds, rivers, glaciers, dinosaurs, and oceans across immense spans of time. If water is constantly recycled, why do some places still face severe water scarcity?
